policy_statement: Fund demonstration projects of CDR technologies to show life-cycle GHG benefits (e.g., direct chemical carbon capture; biomass CDR options like biochar, capture and storage).
mechanism_description: It funds demonstration projects for carbon dioxide removal technologies to demonstrate life-cycle GHG benefits and help enable broader carbon removal deployment.
primary_causal_pathway: Technology deployment support → ↑ CO₂ removed & durably stored (net-negative emissions) → ↓ atmospheric CO₂ → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Public funding de-risks and accelerates pilot/demonstration projects for engineered and hybrid CDR pathways (e.g., DACCS, BECCS, biochar with durable carbon storage), building operational experience, MRV capability, and supply chains. (2) Mediating physical/ecological process: CDR systems capture CO₂ from ambient air or via biogenic uptake and convert/store carbon in durable reservoirs (geologic formations, stable mineralization, long-lived products/soils for biochar) with verified permanence and quantified life-cycle emissions. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in atmospheric CO₂ (after subtracting energy/process and upstream emissions), lowering cumulative GHG forcing.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling scaled carbon dioxide removal that reduces net atmospheric CO₂ concentrations through durable storage.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Demonstration funding → faster CDR scale-up → more CO₂ removed and durably stored → lower net GHG concentrations/forcing.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve “resource efficiency” (learning-by-doing, better process efficiency) and sometimes involves biomass/land management, the defining and largest intended climate effect is direct mitigation via negative emissions (CO₂ removal), not efficiency or hazard reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “CDR” demo is powered by high-carbon energy or lacks durable storage/MRV, net climate benefits could be reduced or negated, but the mechanism as defined is explicitly to demonstrate life-cycle GHG benefits and enable broader removal deployment—still a mitigation mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that funds/hosts CDR technology demonstrations with the goal of enabling scaled carbon removal shares the same primary mitigation pathway (increasing verified removals).  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Whether a given policy instance is engineered vs. biomass-based, high- vs. low-permanence, or coupled to renewables/CCS infrastructure is a policy-specific detail and should be assigned only with policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “CDR demonstrations” may function primarily as R&D capacity building with minimal near-term removals, or may deliver uncertain net-negativity depending on energy source, leakage, and permanence; these affect expected magnitude, not the primary mitigation classification.
key_indicators: "Fund demonstration projects"; "NYSERDA should fund"; "CDR technologies"; "direct chemical carbon capture"; "biochar"; "capture and storage"; "show the GHG benefits"; "life cycle"
co_benefits: Innovation and industry development (clean-tech learning-by-doing, jobs/supply-chain development); improved air quality depending on technology configuration
instance_edge_case_notes: Demonstration funding could be interpreted as part of broader “infrastructure investment” if it included build-out of shared CO₂ transport/storage hubs, but the text focuses on funding pilots/demos (best fit: incentive-program).
